description: Baseline benchmark + focused checks for active-request input latency; benchmark uses synthetic live-thinking churn through the real Textual app.

asi.hypothesis: Establish a representative baseline before touching live thought rendering.
asi.learned: Synthetic active-thinking load produces a clear active-vs-idle gap (~82.8 ms) with p95 latency at 166.5 ms, so the benchmark is sensitive enough to guide UI-churn optimizations.
asi.benchmark_shape: pilot.press() latency during an active request with real app lifecycle and focused UI checks enabled.

description: Removed forced chat scroll_end() on incremental thought-panel updates so live thought refreshes stop driving viewport scrolling on every tick.

asi.hypothesis: The remaining latency is partly caused by the thinking panel forcing a viewport scroll on every incremental update.
asi.learned: In the synthetic active-thinking workload, removing scroll_end() reduced input_p95 from 166.5 ms to 146.4 ms and cut spike severity, so viewport-scroll churn is still a meaningful contributor even after request threading.
asi.user_behavior_preserved: Live thought updates still render; only the incremental forced scroll was removed.

description: Detached the live thinking panel from chat history into a dedicated viewport widget so incremental thought updates no longer mutate chat children during an active request.

asi.hypothesis: Moving live thought updates out of ChatContainer entirely will reduce layout/mount churn beyond the scroll_end removal.
asi.learned: This architectural shift produced essentially no further p95 improvement beyond the simpler scroll_end removal, so the expensive path is likely render/update cost itself rather than child insertion bookkeeping.
asi.rollback_reason: input_p95 regressed slightly versus the current best (146.9 ms vs 146.4 ms) for substantially more complexity.
asi.next_action_hint: Try reducing how often the live thinking Text widget is regenerated, or decouple editor visibility from frequent viewport sibling updates with a cheaper text-only update path.
asi.experiment_type: live thinking widget detached from chat history

description: Coarsened live thought-panel refresh cadence from 100 ms to 200 ms, reducing active-request UI churn while preserving visible streaming thoughts.

asi.hypothesis: The remaining input lag is dominated by too-frequent live thought re-renders; a slightly coarser cadence may preserve the feature while reducing contention.
asi.learned: Doubling thought throttle to 200 ms improved input_p95 to 128.3 ms and shrank the active-vs-idle gap to 62.5 ms, indicating update frequency is a major lever and a 200 ms cadence still keeps live thought updates present.
asi.tradeoff: Thoughts remain live but refresh less often; this is a product tradeoff rather than a hidden/deferred rendering hack.
asi.next_probe: Measure whether 150 ms captures most of the gain with fresher thought updates, or whether adaptive throttling based on editor activity can do even better.

description: Re-ran the 200 ms thought refresh cadence after fixing benchmark lint so the improvement can land cleanly with passing hooks.

asi.hypothesis: The previously measured 200 ms thought cadence win is real; rerunning after trivial benchmark lint cleanup should confirm it and allow a clean keep.
asi.learned: The win reproduced and slightly improved (126.99 ms p95), so the reduced-thought-refresh-cadence change is stable enough to keep.
asi.reason_for_rerun: Previous keep attempts failed due a trivial unused benchmark variable, not due product-code checks.
asi.next_probe: Now that a stable improvement is secured, explore whether adaptive cadence keyed to active editor drafting can reduce p95 further without making thoughts uniformly less fresh.

description: Made thought refresh cadence adaptive: 100 ms normally, 300 ms only while the editor contains an in-progress draft, preserving fresher thoughts when idle while easing typing-time churn.

asi.hypothesis: The best tradeoff may be to slow live thought refreshes only during active drafting rather than all the time.
asi.learned: Adaptive throttling matched/slightly beat the constant-200 ms result (126.15 ms p95) while restoring faster thought updates whenever the user is not typing a draft.
asi.product_tradeoff: Thought visibility stays live; only drafting periods use the slower 300 ms cadence, so non-drafting request observation stays at the original 100 ms cadence.
asi.next_probe: Try a milder drafting cadence (250 ms) or detect actual recent key activity instead of any non-empty editor value to reduce over-throttling after the user pauses.

description: Tried a milder adaptive drafting throttle (250 ms instead of 300 ms) to recover slightly fresher live thoughts while keeping the adaptive behavior.

asi.hypothesis: 250 ms while drafting may preserve most of the latency win with fresher thought updates than 300 ms.
asi.learned: The 250 ms drafting throttle gave back part of the p95 gain versus 300 ms, so 300 ms remains the better responsiveness/freshness tradeoff under this workload.
asi.rollback_reason: Primary metric regressed from 126.15 ms to 126.92 ms with no compensating benefit in the measured workload.
asi.next_action_hint: Keep the 300 ms drafting throttle and explore reducing rerender cost or making the throttle depend on recent key activity rather than any non-empty editor text.

description: Reduced the internal thinking-text buffer from 20k chars to 2.4k chars so live thought updates stop repeatedly copying far more hidden history than the UI can render.

asi.hypothesis: A large hidden thought buffer is wasting time on string growth/copying, because the UI only ever renders the last ~1200 chars anyway.
asi.learned: Cutting the hidden buffer to 2.4k chars improved input_p95 to 124.7 ms and further reduced the active-idle gap, confirming that invisible thought-history retention was contributing measurable churn.
asi.behavior_preserved: Visible thought output is unchanged because the renderer already truncates to 1200 chars with hidden-content markers.
asi.next_probe: Try matching the buffer directly to the visible render budget, or reduce needless re-renders by skipping updates when the rendered panel text is unchanged.

description: Tried shrinking the hidden thought buffer all the way down to the visible 1200-char render budget.

asi.hypothesis: If 2.4k hidden chars helped, matching the buffer exactly to the 1200-char visible budget might cut copy cost further.
asi.learned: The 1200-char buffer regressed versus 2.4k, suggesting a little extra headroom helps avoid pathological churn from constant exact-limit trimming.
asi.rollback_reason: Primary metric regressed from 124.72 ms to 127.02 ms.
asi.next_action_hint: Keep the 2.4k buffer and explore caching/render-diff approaches instead of more aggressive truncation.

description: Tried shrinking the live thought panel's visible char budget to 800 while drafting, while keeping the fuller 1200-char panel on finalize.

asi.hypothesis: Reducing the amount of visible thought text rendered during active drafting might cut rerender cost without hiding live thoughts entirely.
asi.learned: This regressed the primary metric, so render-frequency/buffer-size changes matter more here than the visible char budget itself.
asi.rollback_reason: input_p95 worsened from 124.72 ms to 128.07 ms.
asi.next_action_hint: Keep the existing visible budget and look for cheaper editor-aware throttling or render-skipping heuristics instead of shrinking the live panel payload.

description: Tried a stronger adaptive drafting throttle of 400 ms to see whether even fewer live thought refreshes would materially improve typing responsiveness.

asi.hypothesis: If 300 ms while drafting helped, 400 ms might buy a bit more typing responsiveness without fully hiding thoughts.
asi.learned: 400 ms was slightly worse than the 300 ms drafting throttle once the smaller hidden buffer was in place, so the current optimum appears to be near 300 ms rather than simply 'slower is better'.
asi.rollback_reason: Primary metric regressed from 124.72 ms to 124.97 ms.
asi.next_action_hint: Keep the 300 ms drafting cadence and focus on reducing per-refresh work rather than slowing refreshes further.

description: Reworked the hidden thought buffer from a single growing string into trimmed chunk storage, joining only on render.

asi.hypothesis: Repeated string concatenation on every thought delta may still be expensive; chunk accumulation with throttled joins could reduce per-delta work.
asi.learned: The chunk-buffer approach regressed slightly, so the added bookkeeping/join cost outweighed the saved concatenation under this workload.
asi.rollback_reason: Primary metric regressed from 124.72 ms to 125.72 ms.
asi.next_action_hint: Stick with the simpler bounded-string buffer and focus on lighter-weight editor-side heuristics or direct profiling of Textual input/render timings.

description: Tried deferring queued thought-delta draining until the live thought throttle window opens, so suppressed refresh windows also skip intermediate buffer concatenation work.

asi.hypothesis: Even with adaptive render throttling, draining and appending thought deltas every 100 ms may still waste work; only drain when a render would be allowed.
asi.learned: The idea benchmarked much worse than the current best (145.5 ms p95), so the extra queue retention likely hurt more than it helped even before correctness fixes.
asi.rollback_reason: Focused UI tests failed due to missing time import in new tests, and the primary metric regressed badly anyway.
asi.next_action_hint: Discard this queue-drain deferral path and look for lower-risk wins in measuring or responding to actual recent key activity instead of changing delta delivery semantics.
asi.failure_detail: tests/unit/ui/test_request_threading.py missing time import in new throttle tests

description: Skipped Editor.scroll_to_region() when the cursor is already within the visible editor viewport, to avoid redundant per-keystroke scroll work.

asi.hypothesis: Editor-side scroll bookkeeping may still be adding input latency on every keypress even when no scroll is needed.
asi.learned: This slightly regressed versus the current best, suggesting redundant vertical scrolling is not the dominant remaining cost under the active-thinking workload.
asi.rollback_reason: Primary metric worsened from 124.72 ms to 126.11 ms.
asi.next_action_hint: Keep the simpler editor path and look for thought-render costs or benchmark improvements that capture true local typing pain more directly.

description: Tried amortizing hidden thought-buffer trimming by letting the buffer grow to 3.2k chars before snapping back to the 2.4k steady-state limit.

asi.hypothesis: The current 2.4k cap may cause repeated slice/copy work on every thought append; trimming less often could keep the same visible behavior more cheaply.
asi.learned: This regressed, implying the slightly larger average hidden buffer cost more than the reduced trim frequency saved.
asi.rollback_reason: Primary metric worsened from 124.72 ms to 126.93 ms.
asi.next_action_hint: Keep eager 2.4k trimming and explore render-path improvements rather than looser hidden-buffer retention.
asi.candidate: buffer trim trigger 3200 with 2400 steady limit

description: Tried increasing the shared flush/stream throttle from 100 ms to 150 ms so the UI wakes less often for streaming/thinking work overall.

asi.hypothesis: The remaining latency may partly come from the 100 ms flush cadence itself, not just thought-panel rendering, so a modestly slower global UI delta cadence could help.
asi.learned: Benchmark result stayed worse than the current best, so simply slowing the shared delta timer is not a win under this workload.
asi.rollback_reason: Checks failed because autoresearch.checks.sh still referenced a reverted temporary test file, and the primary metric was not an improvement anyway.
asi.next_action_hint: Restore the checks list to existing focused tests, then keep looking for targeted thought-path wins rather than broader global throttle changes.
asi.failure_detail: autoresearch.checks.sh referenced missing tests/unit/ui/test_thinking_state.py after revert

description: Deferred incremental thought-panel refreshes for 150 ms after the user's most recent editing keypress, so brief typing bursts stay locally responsive while thoughts catch up as soon as typing pauses.

asi.hypothesis: The user-visible pain is concentrated during active typing bursts, so a short post-keypress grace window may help more than a coarser always-on thought cadence.
asi.learned: A 150 ms recent-keypress deferral beat the prior best, improving input_p95 to 123.64 ms and reducing the active-idle gap to 57.9 ms while still allowing the thought panel to render initially and resume after short pauses.
asi.product_tradeoff: Unlike the earlier rejected 'defer while drafting' experiment, this only suppresses incremental updates during active key bursts; non-typing observation still gets live thought updates.
asi.next_probe: Re-run this same change to confirm the sub-1 ms gain over the prior best is real, then consider tuning the defer window slightly if it holds.

description: Re-ran the 150 ms recent-keypress thought deferral after formatting/hooks cleanup; the typing-burst grace window still beats the previous best and now lands with a clean validation path.

asi.hypothesis: The recent-keypress deferral win is real enough to survive a confirmation rerun after hook cleanup.
asi.learned: The rerun stayed better than the pre-change best (124.18 ms vs 124.72 ms), confirming the short typing-burst grace window is a defensible improvement even if the exact peak gain varies run to run.
asi.reason_for_rerun: Previous keep attempt did not auto-commit cleanly despite passing benchmark/checks, so rerunning both validated the change and retried the keep path.
asi.next_probe: Tune the defer window slightly around 150 ms to see whether 125 ms preserves more thought freshness without giving back the responsiveness gain.

description: Tried shortening the recent-keypress thought deferral window from 150 ms to 125 ms to recover slightly fresher live thoughts during short pauses.

asi.hypothesis: A slightly shorter grace window may preserve most of the typing-burst responsiveness gain while letting live thoughts resume sooner.
asi.learned: 125 ms gave back some of the p95 improvement, so the current sweet spot appears closer to 150 ms than 125 ms for this workload.
asi.rollback_reason: Primary metric regressed from 124.18 ms to 124.46 ms.
asi.next_action_hint: Keep the 150 ms grace window and try a slightly longer window or more precise recent-typing detection only if it clearly improves responsiveness without hiding thoughts too long.

description: Tried lengthening the recent-keypress thought deferral window from 150 ms to 175 ms to see whether a bit more burst protection would help typing responsiveness further.

asi.hypothesis: A slightly longer burst grace window might shave a bit more p95 latency while still resuming thoughts quickly after the user pauses.
asi.learned: 175 ms regressed versus the 150 ms setting, so the keyburst deferral optimum appears fairly tight rather than simply 'longer is better'.
asi.rollback_reason: Primary metric regressed from 124.18 ms to 125.04 ms.
asi.next_action_hint: Keep the 150 ms defer window and look for orthogonal wins beyond tuning this knob further.

description: Tried batching thought deltas into a pending buffer during the recent-keypress grace window, then merging them back into the main thought string when rendering resumes.

asi.hypothesis: After suppressing incremental thought renders during typing bursts, avoiding hidden-string growth during the same burst might reduce UI-thread work further.
asi.learned: This regressed sharply, so buffering pending thought deltas separately added more overhead/complexity than the saved string churn was worth.
asi.rollback_reason: Primary metric regressed from 124.18 ms to 131.73 ms.
asi.next_action_hint: Keep only the lightweight recent-keypress render deferral and avoid secondary pending-buffer machinery.
asi.candidate: pending thought delta buffer during recent typing

description: Tried relaxing the coarse drafting-time thought throttle from 300 ms back down to 200 ms while keeping the new 150 ms recent-keypress deferral in place.

asi.hypothesis: The short recent-keypress deferral may carry most of the responsiveness win, allowing fresher between-burst thought updates with a milder drafting throttle.
asi.learned: The 300 ms drafting throttle is still doing major work even after adding recent-keypress deferral; dropping it to 200 ms caused a large regression.
asi.rollback_reason: Primary metric regressed badly from 124.18 ms to 145.22 ms.
asi.next_action_hint: Keep the 300 ms drafting throttle as a necessary companion to the 150 ms recent-keypress grace window.
asi.candidate: drafting throttle 200 ms with 150 ms recent-keypress defer

description: Tried strengthening the coarse drafting-time thought throttle from 300 ms to 350 ms while keeping the 150 ms recent-keypress grace window.

asi.hypothesis: A slightly stronger between-burst drafting throttle might complement the recent-keypress deferral and shave off a bit more contention.
asi.learned: 350 ms regressed, so the current 300 ms drafting throttle remains the better balance even with the added keypress grace window.
asi.rollback_reason: Primary metric regressed from 124.18 ms to 129.20 ms.
asi.next_action_hint: Keep the 300 ms drafting throttle; further gains likely need a different mechanism than more cadence tuning.
asi.candidate: drafting throttle 350 ms with 150 ms recent-keypress defer

description: Tried short-circuiting thought refresh attempts directly in thinking_callback() during recent typing bursts when a panel already exists, instead of entering refresh_thinking_output() just to defer there.

asi.hypothesis: The current recent-keypress deferral still pays some avoidable function/clock-check overhead on each queued thought chunk; an earlier short-circuit might preserve the win and shave a bit more work.
asi.learned: This regressed, so the extra early return likely interfered with useful timing/state flow more than it saved in overhead.
asi.rollback_reason: Primary metric regressed from 124.18 ms to 127.79 ms.
asi.next_action_hint: Keep the existing deferral only inside refresh_thinking_output and avoid over-optimizing that path without direct profiling.
asi.candidate: early thinking_callback short-circuit during recent typing

description: Tried lowering the hidden thought-buffer limit from 2.4k chars to 2.0k chars on top of the current recent-keypress grace window.

asi.hypothesis: With the newer typing-burst deferral in place, a slightly smaller hidden thought buffer might cut hidden-string churn further without hurting visible output.
asi.learned: The 2.0k buffer regressed slightly versus 2.4k, so the earlier 2.4k limit still looks like the better operating point even with the newer keypress deferral.
asi.rollback_reason: Primary metric regressed from 124.18 ms to 124.90 ms.
asi.next_action_hint: Keep the 2.4k hidden buffer and only revisit this if a more representative benchmark points at hidden-string churn again.
asi.candidate: thinking buffer 2000 chars with recent-keypress defer

description: Tried reducing the visible thought panel from 10 lines to 8 lines to cut live render payload without removing the panel entirely.

asi.hypothesis: A modestly smaller live thought panel might reduce render work enough to help typing responsiveness while still keeping thoughts visible.
asi.learned: Even though the active-idle gap narrowed, the primary p95 regressed substantially because idle baseline also worsened; the smaller panel is not a win on the chosen metric.
asi.rollback_reason: Primary metric regressed from 124.18 ms to 126.72 ms.
asi.next_action_hint: Keep the 10-line panel and only revisit visible payload size if a more user-facing benchmark says smaller live thought context is acceptable.

description: Tried using a lighter-weight plain-string live thought render path while keeping the styled Rich panel rendering for the final persisted thought panel.

asi.hypothesis: Per-update Rich `Text` construction may still be a measurable part of live thought-panel cost; a plain-string live path could preserve visuals via CSS with less overhead.
asi.learned: This regressed, so Rich `Text` object construction is not the dominant remaining cost in the live path.
asi.rollback_reason: Primary metric regressed from 124.18 ms to 125.75 ms.
asi.next_action_hint: Keep the simpler existing render path and avoid splitting live/final thought rendering without stronger profiling evidence.

description: Baseline for the new keypress-to-render benchmark: current branch state measured against editor paint latency rather than coarse pilot.press wall time.

asi.hypothesis: Switch to a more user-facing benchmark before continuing tuning, so further changes target visible local typing latency instead of only tool-harness wall time.
asi.learned: On the current best branch state, editor paint p95 is already much lower than pilot.press wall time (22.9 ms vs 127.7 ms), which suggests the user pain may live in burst stalls/outliers or in phenomena the old coarse benchmark hid.
asi.benchmark_change: Primary metric now records time until Editor.render_line runs with the updated value.
asi.previous_best_context: Branch already contains earlier wins: scroll_end removal, adaptive drafting throttle, smaller hidden thought buffer, and 150 ms recent-keypress deferral.
